models.reason: Bibee’s underlying skill and recent shape are better than Fedde’s, and Cleveland also has the cleaner bullpen outlook with Chicago’s leverage group heavily thinned. The problem is Cleveland’s weak run-production baseline versus righties, which keeps my fair price short of a bet threshold. I project the total slightly above 8.5 because Fedde and the taxed White Sox pen can leak late runs, but not by enough to stake it.

CHW’s run projection dominates behind Bibee’s L14 surge blending to a 3.86 SIERA while Fedde’s 5.15 SIERA and CLE’s league‑worst .292 wOBA vs RHP (wRC+ 92) create a wide gap. Bullpen edge also favors CHW — CLE missing closer Smith adds 0.30 runs to the host’s expected tally, lifting CHW to a 69.2% win probability against an implied 47%.

The Chicago bullpen is heavily taxed with only one high-leverage arm fresh, triggering a +1.50 bullpen ERA penalty. Coupled with Erick Fedde's high 5.15 SIERA aggregate and warm, outward-blowing winds, our model projects 9.70 runs and a clear edge on the Over.

Boston has the better starter, the far better platoon split versus a lefty, and the more trustworthy bullpen, while Colorado’s lineup profile versus lefties is poor. Even so, Coors inflates variance enough that I will not force a moneyline at this favorite price. My total projection is above 11, but Coors is exactly the kind of wide-distribution environment where I want more cushion before betting.

Tampa has the better overall game-state because Kansas City’s bullpen is badly depleted, but the Rays’ weak split versus lefties keeps the moneyline from clearing my side gate. The over is the more interesting angle because KC has been hitting and its tired bullpen can turn a five-inning game into an eight-run game quickly. Still, my adjusted projection does not quite get far enough past 8.0 to bet.

Cameron’s crafty lefty profile and TB’s league‑worst .289 wOBA vs LHP (wRC+ 89) drive a 2-run KC advantage. Tampa Bay’s missing closer Baker adds another 0.30 runs to KC’s ledger, and the market has barely moved, leaving a 17.5-pt gap even after a small-sample discount on Jax.
This is a near 50/50 matchup between two solid lefties, but the market heavily favors Tampa Bay. Getting Kansas City at +131 represents a clear overlay with a 6.2 percentage point edge.

Cameron and Jax are a dead heat by SIERA, and the Royals’ taxed bullpen (1.5 freshness score) faces a rested Tampa pen (2.25). My combined run estimate is 9.2 against an 8.0 line, but both starters carry [sm] recent flags; under the small-sample rule the wider confidence band straddles no-edge, forcing a lean only.

I do not have enough separation to lay the Phillies price because Nola’s true level is better than his ERA, but not enough better to create a strong side number against a live Washington offense. The total is cleaner: both teams project for offense versus right-handed pitching, Mikolas is the weaker starter, Washington’s bullpen is light in setup depth, and the park/weather combo is favorable. My regressed total lands around 10.4, which is enough to play over 9.5 at the available price.

Mikolas’ extreme ground-ball tilt (53.4% GB) pairs well with WAS’s top‑10 defense vs a PHI offense that has been league‑average over the last 10 games. Nola’s regression (4.19 SIERA L14) and WAS’s big platoon edge vs RHP (109 wRC+) against a tired PHI bullpen give the home dog a 54.3% win probability, 9 points above market.

PHI's red-hot L10 offense and WAS's home park factor push the projected total well above the market line. Nola's recent L14 regression and Mikolas's lack of swing-and-miss stuff (Stf+ 91) further support the over, securing my second and final total bet of the slate.

Toronto is fairly priced to slightly expensive for me because Houston’s offense versus righties is strong enough to keep the side from becoming a play even with the Astros’ bullpen in rough shape. The total is the better angle: Burrows is a weak baseline starter, Yesavage’s recent shape has slipped, Houston’s relief availability is poor, and the offensive splits point toward sustained scoring chances on both sides. My adjusted projection is 9.7, giving the over a clear cushion above 8.5.

A taxed Houston bullpen is penalized under Phase 2, inflating the expected run allowance. With both starting pitchers carrying aggregate SIERAs above 4.40, our model projects 9.80 total runs, creating a solid 1.3-run edge on the Over.

Milwaukee is the better team, but Cincinnati’s strong split versus left-handed pitching keeps the side market close enough to fair that I will not pay Brewers chalk. The total has multiple independent drivers: Milwaukee’s elite offense versus a weak righty, Cincinnati’s capable split versus lefties, two starters whose estimators are worse than their surface lines, and a warm Great American environment that amplifies homer risk. My regressed projection is 10.3, so over 9.0 at plus money is a clear bet.

This fires my non-viable-favorite logic in reverse: Lowder is functionally a bullpen game (AGG SIERA 5.11, K-BB% 6.0%, Stf+ 85) while Drohan's AGG is thinner but his form/skill grades far better, and MIL's vs-RHP wRC+ 123 lineup is the slate's best matchup edge. My fair MIL ~ -176 (63.9%) vs market no-vig ~58.8%, a 5.1-pt gap clearing my gate. Lowder being a dog-quality arm priced as a competitive home side is the misprice. Total xR ≈ 9.6 vs 9.0 — Over lean only, below gate, and I won't double-stack correlated exposure.

The Dodgers have the better full-team profile, but Joe Ryan is good enough to keep the moneyline from being attractive at a heavy favorite price. The total is lower than a pure run-baseline model would suggest because both offenses are dangerous, yet two strong starters and a modest park keep me from forcing the over. I make it only slightly above market after regression, so it stays a lean.

Ohtani and Ryan own shiny ERAs, but their blended xFIP profiles (3.87 and 3.12) are merely good against two top-quartile offenses. Minnesota’s bullpen is taxed (0 of 1 T1 fresh, 1.5 score) and Target Field’s wind is carrying. I estimate 8.9 combined runs, 1.4 above the 7.5 total.

Ryan Weathers (3.38 aggregate SIERA) matches up beautifully against a weak Tigers offense, while the Yankees' elite lineup represents a major upgrade over Detroit's. Our model projects NYY with a 53.6% win probability, making them a high-conviction 3-unit play at a generous underdog price.

Both offenses feature elite contact quality (HH% > 38%, Brl% > 7.5%) against two starters whose recent L14 xFIPs are regressing worse than their season aggregates. The market total is severely mispriced given the run environment, yielding a massive edge that justifies a max 3-unit play.

Jump and Mahle are contact-oriented starters with below-average strike-zone dominance (Mahle Stf+ 89, Jump K-BB% 14.4%) facing lineups both above 110 wRC+ in the platoon split. Oakland’s taxed bullpen (0 of 2 T1 fresh, 1.5 score) will leak late runs, while Oracle’s short RF porch and west wind help power. I estimate 9.2 combined runs.

LAA's side edge is kept below the gate by BAL's strong platoon offense and the small-sample nature of both starters' recent work. The total projects slightly under the market line, but the -120 price fails my staking threshold, resulting in a lean.
